speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING  YIDDISH] You understand Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: She does not understand Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: They spit in my face. I am not going to say it's raining, ever. You call a spade a spade.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had a woman yesterday. She was from Romania. She came in '63 only to United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH] Look at you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date October 29, 1994, Barbara Drotow, survivor, interviewer, Mary Rothschild, city, West Hollywood, the language, English. First tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is October 29, 1994. I'm interviewing Barbara Drotow for the Survivors of Shoah Foundation. And my name is Mary Rothschild. Please tell us your name before the war, the date of your birth, and the place you were born, and a little bit about your family life before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is-- I was born June 1, 1933, in Wloclawek on the Wisla in Poland. When I was three months old, I, my family moved to Lodz. And there I lived till we left Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, my name was [? Bronja ?] Kenig. We, I lived with my mother and father and a sister. My father's family, they were 10 brothers and two sisters. Only one, only one brothers, two brothers survived. They are very old. They are now in New York. The eight brothers, including my, my father, and the two sisters, and the entire family was wiped out. They all lived in Kutno and in Lodz. My mother's family, they were three sisters and four brothers. My mother's maiden name was [? Eiche ?]. Nobody survived. The entire family was wiped out. There were the-- my mom was born in [POLISH], next, not too far from Lodz. Her brothers and sisters lived in Lodz. Nobody survived. There's nobody. We were a big family. And I don't remember too much from before the war. It's just, I really don't remember. I know that we lived in, in the center of Lodz. And my mom was a designer. Here, she would be called a designer. And she was an excellent seamstress. My dad was a businessman. And I guess that we had a factory on, in Lodz. There is not too much that I remember right before the war. But when, when the war started in September, 1939, I know-- I remember that they were talking there is a war. And the Germans are coming. And it's like in, in a dream. I do remember it. And all I know that one day, my mommy was crying. And, and my sister was crying. And I started crying. And my father did not come back home. And there was a roundup. And we, he never came back. And we didn't know where he is. This was right, I think, two or three months after the Germans came to, to Lodz. And then-- this I don't remember, but my sister told me-- that my uncle Chaim, which was my, the brother of my mother, they went to [? Bawuti, ?] this which was a marketplace in Lodz. And he was among the first Jews that were hanged in the market. He was one of the first Jews that were hung. They were hung in Lodz, in the market. This my sister told me. Soon enough, there's something that I'm telling that I don't remember too much, but my sister related it to me. People were scared. They started throwing out people from the better neighborhoods, like [? Pyotrkovska ?]. My, one of my aunts, she lived [? Zavotska ?]. And she came to live with us, [? Chochatola. ?] And things were happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that my mother was always crying. And [? Chochatola ?] was crying. And they say, what's going to happen to us? And then-- How old were you, Barbara?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1933, so I was around six years old, something like this. So there are some things that I do remember and some that I don't remember. I guess we also have selective selective memory. But if it's frightful, I, I really don't remember. But I do remember that there was a lot of crying. And that we ate [SPEAKING YIDDISH], which is dry bread. That there, and my mother would go and my sister. And they would run to the bakery and bring bread. Then I don't know what happened to my-- and I don't remember what happened-- oh, my sister told me that my [? Chochatola ?] was left in the apartment which we had. And then, my sister said that we are going to go away. And I shouldn't tell anybody. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] Don't tell it to anybody. My moth-- this is related to me by my sister. And I'm relating it to you. We had a good friend. And they were friends from, my, my parent's friend. His name was Mietek. And he was a Volksdeutsche. He was a Polish citizen, but he was from German descent. And they were very close friend through the years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the Germans came, he became a policeman. For what reason? I-- maybe just to save his life. And he was in the police, in the German police.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said they started making the ghetto, which was around, I think, May. And he said, Mina, he said to my mother, things are bad. Things are bad. Don't ask me anymore. And he said, I'm going to smuggle you out. So get-- if you have some valuables, take them. Dress the children warm, the two girls. It was my sister and I. We didn't know where my dad is. We assumed that he's dead. And he said, I'll smuggle you out at night. Don't tell it even to your sister, [? Tola. ?] I'll say that you are coming to my house, just to eat and whatever. And that's what happened. He came at night. He took-- I remember, I, I was holding on to my mother's hand and my sister. And we were dressed warm. And we went to his house, because it was at night, late in the evening. And we never came back to the house there. And he took us in the wagon with horses. And we were-- and he said, put the children down. And my mother was dressed as a, as a, as a, as a Polish peasant. And he was a policeman, so I don't know how he did it. Maybe he gave, he bribed some people. And my mother gave him the valuables that she could not take with her. She gave him the keys of the factory, and which he took over. And she gave him the papers and everything. And she signed over everything to him. And he smuggled us out. We, he brought us to Warsaw. And from there, all I know that there were a lot of people already in Warsaw. All I know that I was always holding on to my sister. We were sleeping in some barracks. And then another person took us from Warsaw till the border, again, in a wagon where we were laying down under the straw till the border, by this time till the border of Russia. By this time, because Germ-- the Russians occupied-- this was I don't know exactly what year. Yeah. This was the Germans, the Germans had most of Poland. And the Russians had eastern Poland that was on the border of Russia. And this I remember, that my mother and my sis-- my sister, she was the one that was telling me. To her, I was listening. And she said, if you will cry or if [INAUDIBLE], she said, I'll kill you. I'll pinch you to, to death. Don't make a noise, because they will kill us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your sister, Barbara?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luba is six years older than I. So she was around, close to 12. And she, and I was, she was my god. Whatever my sister said, I listened to her. And we were let out of in the middle of the night. The Polack, the Polish peasant let us out. And we were left near the border. And we were trying to cross it, to cross the Bug, the River Bug. And my mother took us by the hand. And we didn't know how to swim, but there was still ice on the Bug. It was in the winter. It was on the border. I mean, it was April. And we fell in. And my sister pulled out my mother. And my mother fell in. I was somehow, I remember. And she fell in, and she pulled her out. And we found a-- like walking on ice. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And finally, we came to the, to the other side. We came to the other side. And I think the phone is ringing.

speaker: interviewer
text: We start again. It's take one, start two.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I don't know whether I mentioned it. And so I'll go back. When he smuggled us out, they started making the ghetto. And that was the reason that he smuggled us out, because he said, it's bad. It's really bad. So he probably knew much more than the people knew, because a lot of people didn't want to leave their houses and their apartments. And he said, leave everything. I'm going to smuggle you out. Things are really bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. Oh. yes. So finally, we got out of the water from the ice. And we, we were soaking wet. And we started. And he told, the peasant said, walk this direction. And he told my mother. So my mother took me by her hand and my sister by her hand. And she said, whatever it is, let's hold on. So we came to-- we came, all of a sudden, we saw some lights. And people started screaming. And these were the Russian border guards. That's what my sister told me. And they say, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], go back, go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was talking to them in Polish. There are Germans there. We have been smuggled out. Please, let's us this-- they said, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. And they pushed us back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother said, I'm not going to go. So we were sitting for awhile on the, at the river's edge. And then, we went again. And some other patrol met us. And they saw my mother and two children, say, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. I guess that they were also afraid, the soldiers. And look at those, those people. This is a woman with two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they pushed us back. So and my mother was pleading. She said, we are wet. My children are, will, will die from-- if we didn't die there, we will die here from pneumonia. And but they told us to go back. But I guess my mother was very tenacious. She, we, we rested for a while. And then she said, she, we were all huddling. We found a-- I don't remember it, but my sister tells. We were all huddling under some straw. And we went again to the border. And, and the same patrol met us. And they let us through, so we came. And we were walked. I remember, we were walking for a long, long time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in April. So it was quite cold yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940, yes. And it was, you see, I don't remember the-- and we were walking for a few days. We were also going to some Polish villages. Some, some peasants were very nice. And my mother, I think she had some valuables in her-- she sold some valuables. And she was giving it to them. So they fed us. And they gave us clean, some warm clothing, clothing. And we were walking for quite a few days. And all I know that we wound up in a city called Bialystok, which was the western, I think, Belarussia. And till we came to the city of Bialystok. And in Bialystok, we, the Russians were there already. And the border patrol, which was very nice, they gave a little note that this is a woman with two children, and let her through. And my mother-- and then we found out in letter, too, she's looking for her husband. And some people told her that he might be in Russia, because we didn't know what happened to our father. And we hoped maybe he survived. Maybe he run away, especially that somebody said that they think that they saw him, that they heard that Chaim Kenig run away from the Germans when there was a roundup and that he's someplace in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed in Bialystok. And we stayed there for about three months. And my mother was going to the, to the Russian authorities to ask maybe they saw-- whether they know about a person that is in Russia. His name is so and so. And lo and behold, after three months, they came. Because she gave all the details about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were living also while we were in Bialystok, we were living in one little room. And we got some cards for bread and for things like this. But we were eating already, you know. And then one day, my sister said, we found our father. We found our father. Papa is alive. And he's in a little town in the Ukraine near Kiev. And so they, a lot of people that we lived, when we lived in Bialystok, there were a lot of people, Jewish people that run away. And then, they didn't want to stay in Russia. They said, it's not good here. We will go back. The Germans probably won't-- some people that run away right away in 1939 thought that the Germans are the Germans from 1914, especially the older ones. And they went back to the cities, back to Poland. We didn't go back, because we thought that, we knew that, we were looking for our father. And most of the people went, some, the group that we were with went back to Lodz and to other cities in Poland. And you know what happened to them. They all went up in smoke. But we stayed in Bialystok. And when we found our father, we, we knew that he's in a little city. And we were crying and hugging. I remember, again, my sister, my mother were crying. And the Russian people gave us some tickets there. And we went to the city next to where my father lived. I don't remember too good, though I was six years old. My mother, I remember a little bit better. She had long hair, I remember. And she had them in braids. But I do remember my dad, too. And we were in a little city in, in Russia where my dad also, also lived. All I know that I was happy. There was a house there, a room. And we lived there. And, and I started going to school, I think the first grade. I, I started to learn. And then all of sudden, one morning-- this was in 1941-- I don't remember too good what was in Russia. But I know that my dad was alive. We met each-- I mean, and my mom was with us and my sister. My sister said she's going to a school. And she was teaching me a little bit Russian. And then, one day, there was crying. And we heard explosions. And this must have been June, 1941, when the Russian invaded, and they also invaded the Ukraine. And my sister said, all I saw that we start packing things again. And they said, we have to run away. The Germans occupied Russia. And they starting to bomb Kiev. And they were bombing. I heard explosions, so they were bombing already the little city. Because it was close to, to Kiev. And we started again running again. And I remember that we just grabbed a few things, because the city was being bombarded. And we went with the other hundreds and thousands of refugees that were going eastward, and because the Ru-- the, the German army was advancing very fast. And I remember, we were huddling in the, in the train. And I remember my mother was holding me. And my, my sister was next to me. And my dad and my mom were sitting. And they were bombing the places. And then I remember for weeks, we were in a train. And people were running out for hot water. And they were bombing-- for days, we were in train. And they were-- bombs were falling. And then, the bombing stopped. And we were in the train till we were four weeks till we came to a place-- for weeks and weeks. So because the Russian army was advancing. And we came to a, to a place. And we were there for a couple of weeks. I don't remember, but my sister tells me. And then they said, we are going to a safe place where it's going to be in Ural, almost in Siberia. And that's where we stayed there, for-- I remember. And I do remember. I remember cows. And we stayed there for about four or five, four or five months. And then, my dad came. And he -- it was in a village. And my mom-- my sister was always the one that was telling me the things. And she said, we're going to go to, to Tashkent. This was in Uzbekistan. And it's warm over there. And there are a lot of Jewish people are going there. And it's safer. And it's nice and warm. Because here, we cannot stay here. This is a temporary place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came till-- again, we were in the train for weeks and for weeks. And because the-- and as I said, why are we going again? This is a nice place. And I remember that my sister told me, the Germans are moving forward. They might even-- they might occupy Moscow. So the people from Moscow and from Kiev and everybody is running to Siberia. And this is maybe a safe place. But Siberia is not safe anymore. So we will go to Uzebekistan. That's where it's warm. And now, I rationalize, I, that's, that's why we were running away, because the Germans were advancing towards Moscow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to Tashkent. And from then, they told us to-- I mean, we were assigned to a small village in Uzbekistan. And it was called-- I don't remember what it was. But the main city was called Andijan. And we were living in a small village with the, the Uzbeks there. And we had some food and everything. And then, and I guess my mother got pregnant when she met, when they were reunited in, in the little town. And my mo-- I know that my sister was telling me, we're going to have a baby. We're going to have a new baby. And my, my dad was there. I mean, he-- and then one day, my mother was crying very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, why is mama crying? She said, papa is going. They are taking him away. He has to go to the army. So they took him away, because I guess that the Russian army needed a lot of people. Because the Germans were advancing. And it was-- and then I know I had a little brother. And my sister-- my mother gave birth. I don't know how. All I know that they told me to go out. And she had the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a little brother. And she said, now we have a brother. And they named him, they named him Leibel, after my grandfather that we knew that he probably was killed, because he was left in Kutno. And we lived there. We didn't hear nothing from our father anymore. But my mother, my sister, and I-- and I was already six and a half or seven, close to seven. And we were, when my dad left the village, we started a-- there was hunger in there. I didn't-- because people were getting rations. And we were not as hungry, because my mother was a-- here, she would be called a designer. She used to design. She like, she would draw. And then she would cut. And she would.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was sewing for people, for the women, the, the village women. Those, and they were Muslim women, because this was in Uzbekistan. And she was sewing for them, so they would bring sometimes bread or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, my mother was nursing the baby. And then, again, my, my mother started getting sick. She got very sick. And then she couldn't work anymore, sew the things for, for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister was crying. She said, I think mama is going to die. She's very sick. So she said, listen-- and then, the baby got sick. And I said, why? And because my mother couldn't nurse the baby. He was close, Leibel was close to a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, then my mother got very sick. And my sister said, listen, stay here. Stay here with Leibel. Don't, don't go no place. And some other Russia-- the Uzbek women helped her. And they put her in a wagon. And they brought her to the city close to, to Andijan. Because we were in a small village. And my sister brought her there. And Luba was close to 13, so she was, you know, she was, she was feisty. She was always feisty. And she brought her to the city. And then, she came back. She said, I brought mama there. They'll give her medicine. And we will stay here. And we have to take care of the baby. And then, my mother was bartering. She was selling. And people would give her money-- I mean, food. Because money-- there was no-- you worked for bartering. And our breadwinner was sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my baby, our baby started getting sick. And I said, Luba, why is the baby sick? She said, because he has no food. Mama cannot nurse him. She cannot give him milk. ,

speaker: interviewee
text: And then then one day, my mother was still sick in the [? heart. ?] My sister would go 12 kilometers to the city. And always, and always, she would come back. And she would say, mama is not well yet. And I don't know how many weeks it took, but it took close to a month. And one day, she came back. And no, my baby died first. She was still in the hospital. And the baby was-- I guess if you don't get food, if you stop all of a sudden nursing at close to a year-- he was a couple of months old. He was I think 10 or 11 months, my sister told me. And he started getting sick. And he lethargic. And I would say, how come the-- so we would give him our-- we had a little bit--
